title: Shared Responsibility
question: What does the cloud shared responsibility model mean?
options: Provider and customer split security responsibilities depending on service type
The provider handles every customer security task
The customer owns physical datacenter security
No one is responsible for backups
correctAnswer: Provider and customer split security responsibilities depending on service type
explanation: Cloud providers secure infrastructure, while customers secure their data/configuration depending on IaaS/PaaS/SaaS. The provider does not handle every task, customers do not secure provider datacenters, and backups still need ownership.
sourceLine: What does the cloud shared responsibility model mean?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Object Storage
question: Which workload is object storage like Amazon S3 best suited for?
options: Storing images, backups, logs, and static assets as objects
Running CPU instructions directly
Replacing RAM
Managing process scheduling
correctAnswer: Storing images, backups, logs, and static assets as objects
explanation: Object storage stores blobs with metadata at scale. It does not execute CPU instructions, replace RAM, or schedule OS processes.
sourceLine: Which workload is object storage like Amazon S3 best suited for?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Auto Scaling Trigger
question: Why is auto scaling used in cloud systems?
options: To adjust capacity based on demand
To disable monitoring
To make all servers stateful
To remove the need for load balancing always
correctAnswer: To adjust capacity based on demand
explanation: Auto scaling adds or removes capacity as load changes. It requires monitoring, often benefits from stateless design, and commonly works with load balancers.
sourceLine: Why is auto scaling used in cloud systems?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: IaaS vs PaaS
question: Which statement correctly describes PaaS compared with IaaS?
options: PaaS abstracts more infrastructure management from developers
PaaS always gives root access to physical hosts
IaaS removes all server management
SaaS and IaaS are identical
correctAnswer: PaaS abstracts more infrastructure management from developers
explanation: PaaS manages more runtime/platform concerns so developers focus on apps. It usually does not expose physical root, IaaS still requires more server management, and SaaS/IaaS differ significantly.
sourceLine: Which statement correctly describes PaaS compared with IaaS?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Regions and Availability Zones
question: Why deploy across multiple availability zones?
options: To improve resilience against zone-level failure
To reduce authentication requirements
To make backups unnecessary
To force all traffic through one server
correctAnswer: To improve resilience against zone-level failure
explanation: Multiple AZs reduce impact from a single zone outage. They do not remove auth, backups, or require single-server traffic.
sourceLine: Why deploy across multiple availability zones?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Eventual Consistency
question: What does eventual consistency mean?
options: Replicas may temporarily differ but converge if no new updates occur
Data is always immediately identical everywhere
Writes are impossible
Only one server can read data
correctAnswer: Replicas may temporarily differ but converge if no new updates occur
explanation: Eventual consistency allows temporary divergence for availability/performance. Strong immediate equality is strong consistency, and reads/writes are still possible.
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Zero-Downtime Deployment
question: Which practice best supports zero-downtime deployment?
options: Rolling or blue-green deployment with health checks
Stopping all servers before release
Manual database edits in production only
Disabling monitoring during deploy
correctAnswer: Rolling or blue-green deployment with health checks
explanation: Rolling/blue-green releases keep serving traffic while validating healthy instances. Stopping all servers causes downtime, manual DB edits are risky, and monitoring should stay enabled.
sourceLine: Which practice best supports zero-downtime deployment?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.

title: Cloud Cost Control
question: Which action most directly helps control unexpected cloud cost?
options: Budgets, alerts, right-sizing, and shutting down unused resources
Giving admin access to everyone
Ignoring logs
Using only one region forever
correctAnswer: Budgets, alerts, right-sizing, and shutting down unused resources
explanation: Cost control needs visibility and resource hygiene. Broad admin access increases risk, ignoring logs hides waste, and one region alone does not solve cost issues.
sourceLine: Which action most directly helps control unexpected cloud cost?
referenceNote: Curated professional MCQ. Each option is designed to test a specific interview concept.
